prompt: What is the most reliable immediate indicator that bag-valve-mask ventilations are effective?

options.text: Visible chest rise with each ventilation
Gurgling sounds only
Patient ability to speak full sentences during BVM
Cyanosis becoming deeper

explanation: Adequate BVM produces chest rise and improved oxygenation indicators; speaking during BVM means the seal/technique is wrong.

distractorExplanations.c: Patients cannot speak full sentences during proper BVM seal.

prompt: An adult patient with suspected opioid overdose is unresponsive with slow, shallow respirations. After scene safety and airway opening, the priority intervention is:

options.text: Encourage the patient to walk it off
Support ventilation with BVM/oxygen and consider naloxone per protocol
Give oral fluids
Apply a tourniquet to both arms

explanation: Respiratory depression drives opioid morbidity; ventilate and reverse per protocol.

distractorExplanations.d: Tourniquets are for hemorrhage, not overdose.

prompt: Which oxygen delivery device typically provides the highest FiO2 for a spontaneously breathing patient when used correctly?

options.text: Nasal cannula at 1 L/min
Simple face mask at 2 L/min
Nonrebreather mask at adequate flow
Room air only

explanation: A properly flushed nonrebreather at high flow delivers high FiO2 versus low-flow nasal cannula.

distractorExplanations.b: 2 L is inadequate for a simple mask and still lower than NRB.

prompt: Stridor in an upper-airway obstruction patient suggests:

options.text: Normal quiet breathing
Only lower-lobe pneumonia
Cardiac tamponade exclusively
Partial obstruction at or above the larynx producing high-pitched inspiratory sound

explanation: Stridor is a hallmark of upper airway narrowing and is an emergency warning sign.

distractorExplanations.c: Tamponade presents with shock/Beck-type findings, not stridor.

prompt: When suctioning an adult airway, a key safety limit is:

options.text: Limit suction attempts to about 15 seconds and preoxygenate as able
Suction continuously for several minutes without pause
Never suction if vomit is present
Suction only after intubation in all cases

explanation: Prolonged suction causes hypoxia; clear the airway in brief attempts.

distractorExplanations.c: Vomit is a primary indication to suction.
distractorExplanations.d: BLS suction may be needed before advanced airway.

prompt: The head-tilt/chin-lift is generally avoided in trauma patients because:

options.text: It always causes vomiting
It may worsen cervical spine injury; use jaw-thrust when indicated
It increases blood pressure too much
It is illegal in all EMS systems

explanation: Suspected c-spine injury warrants jaw-thrust with spinal motion restriction considerations.

distractorExplanations.a: Vomiting is not the primary reason.
distractorExplanations.c: BP effect is not the rationale.
distractorExplanations.d: It is standard for medical unresponsiveness without trauma concern.

prompt: Wheezing is most commonly associated with:

options.text: Complete apnea
Only pneumothorax
Lower airway bronchoconstriction such as asthma/COPD exacerbations
Hyperglycemia alone

explanation: Expiratory wheeze reflects narrowed lower airways; still assess for other causes.

distractorExplanations.a: Apnea has no breath sounds of wheeze.
distractorExplanations.d: Glucose disorders do not directly cause wheeze.

prompt: A patient with inadequate breathing should receive:

options.text: Only a nasal cannula regardless of effort
No airway interventions until hospital
Abdominal thrusts if they can speak clearly
Assisted positive-pressure ventilation (e.g., BVM) rather than passive oxygen alone

explanation: Inadequate rate/depth needs assisted ventilation; oxygen alone does not ventilate.

distractorExplanations.c: Speaking patients are not complete airway obstruction candidates for thrusts.

prompt: CPAP in EMS (when indicated and available) primarily helps patients with:

options.text: Respiratory distress from conditions like CHF/pulmonary edema by stenting airways and improving oxygenation
Complete airway obstruction from a foreign body
Cardiac arrest without breathing
Unresponsive patients without a pulse

explanation: CPAP requires a spontaneously breathing cooperative patient; not for arrest or complete obstruction.

distractorExplanations.d: Pulseless patients need resuscitation, not CPAP.

prompt: Cyanosis indicates:

options.text: Always perfect oxygenation
Significant hypoxemia and need for urgent airway/oxygen/ventilation assessment
Only anemia with normal oxygen
Hyperthermia exclusively

explanation: Central cyanosis is a late, serious sign of poor oxygenation.

prompt: A patient with severe asthma is wheezing less than before and becoming drowsy with poor air movement. This most likely indicates:

options.text: Complete recovery
That nebulizer therapy is no longer needed ever
Worsening obstruction and impending respiratory failure
A primary psychiatric emergency only

explanation: Silent chest/fatigue with drowsiness signals exhaustion and failure—support ventilation and ALS.

distractorExplanations.a: Less wheezing can mean less air movement, not cure.

prompt: The preferred method to open the airway of a trauma patient with possible c-spine injury is:

options.text: Head-tilt/chin-lift without spinal precautions
Flexing the neck fully forward
Placing a pillow under the head aggressively
Jaw-thrust maneuver while maintaining manual cervical stabilization

explanation: Jaw-thrust minimizes cervical movement in trauma airway opening.

distractorExplanations.a: Head-tilt may move the cervical spine.
distractorExplanations.b: Flexion is harmful in c-spine injury.
distractorExplanations.c: Pillows can flex/extend the neck improperly.

prompt: Adequate adult ventilation rate with a BVM for a patient with a pulse (general guideline concept) is approximately:

options.text: About 1 breath every 5–6 seconds (roughly 10–12/min), enough to produce visible chest rise
60 breaths per minute as fast as possible
One breath every 30 seconds only
No ventilation if SpO2 is unknown

explanation: Avoid hyperventilation; deliver breaths that produce chest rise at an appropriate rate.

prompt: Suctioning an adult patient's airway should be limited to approximately:

options.text: Several continuous minutes without pause
No more than about 15 seconds per attempt while monitoring oxygenation
Only after the patient is fully cyanotic
Never, because suction removes oxygen permanently

explanation: Brief suction clears the airway; prolonged suction worsens hypoxia—reoxygenate between attempts.

distractorExplanations.c: Do not wait for severe cyanosis.

prompt: A nonrebreather mask is most appropriate when:

options.text: The patient is apneic and needs artificial ventilation
The patient requires precise low-flow FiO2 titration only like a Venturi without assessment
A breathing patient needs high-concentration oxygen and can tolerate the mask
You want to withhold oxygen from hypoxia

explanation: NRB delivers high FiO2 to spontaneously breathing patients; apneic patients need BVM/advanced airway support.

distractorExplanations.b: NRB is high-flow/high FiO2, not precision low-flow alone.

prompt: For an unresponsive adult with no normal breathing and no pulse, the immediate action is:

options.text: Begin high-quality CPR and apply AED as soon as available
Give two minutes of detailed history first
Wait for ALS before any compressions
Check blood glucose before CPR

explanation: Cardiac arrest care prioritizes compressions and early defibrillation.

distractorExplanations.c: BLS CPR must not wait for ALS.

prompt: High-quality adult CPR compression depth is approximately:

options.text: 0.5 inch only
At least 2 inches (5 cm) while allowing full chest recoil
4–5 inches every compression
No depth target exists

explanation: AHA-aligned adult depth is at least 2 inches with full recoil and adequate rate.

distractorExplanations.c: Excessive depth risks injury without benefit targets.

prompt: An AED advises a shock. You should:

options.text: Check a pulse for a full minute before shocking
Turn the AED off and start driving
Ensure no one is touching the patient, deliver the shock, then immediately resume CPR
Place the pads over a medication patch without removing it

explanation: Clear, shock, then immediate CPR minimizes interruptions.

distractorExplanations.d: Remove patches and wipe residue before pads.

prompt: Aspirin is given in suspected ACS (per protocol) primarily because it:

options.text: Instantly dissolves all coronary plaques
Raises blood sugar for energy
Replaces the need for EMS transport
Inhibits platelet aggregation and may limit clot progression

explanation: Antiplatelet effect of aspirin is a key early ACS treatment when not contraindicated.

distractorExplanations.a: It does not dissolve plaques instantly.
distractorExplanations.b: It is not a glucose therapy.

prompt: Nitroglycerin is contraindicated when:

options.text: The patient has used a PDE-5 inhibitor recently or has hypotension per protocol
The patient has chest pain
Systolic BP is 150 mm Hg
The patient is on aspirin

explanation: Nitroglycerin can cause profound hypotension with PDE-5 drugs or low BP.

distractorExplanations.b: Chest pain is an indication when allowed.
distractorExplanations.c: 150 systolic is not a typical contraindication.

prompt: Right-sided heart failure commonly presents with:

options.text: Only left-sided crackles with no edema ever
Peripheral edema and JVD from systemic congestion
Hypoglycemia as the primary sign
Stridor

explanation: Right-sided failure backs up into the systemic veins; left-sided often causes pulmonary edema.

distractorExplanations.a: Left failure more often causes pulmonary findings.
distractorExplanations.c: Glucose is unrelated as primary HF sign.

prompt: During CPR, the compression-to-ventilation ratio for adult 1-rescuer BLS is:

options.text: 15:2 only for all adults
100:0 always

explanation: Adult BLS uses 30 compressions to 2 breaths for 1 or 2 rescuers without advanced airway.

distractorExplanations.d: Continuous compressions apply with advanced airway.

prompt: Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) is suggested by:

options.text: Asystole on the monitor
Absence of any end-tidal CO2 forever
Fixed dilated pupils only
Return of organized pulse/signs of circulation after arrest care

explanation: ROSC means circulation returns; continue post-arrest support and transport.

prompt: Chest pain described as pressure with radiation to the arm/jaw in an adult should be treated as:

options.text: Possible acute coronary syndrome until proven otherwise
Always simple muscle strain
Only anxiety in men under 70
A reason to refuse oxygen forever

explanation: Classic ACS symptoms warrant prompt EMS cardiac protocols.

distractorExplanations.c: ACS occurs across ages/sexes with varied presentations.
distractorExplanations.d: Oxygen is protocol-based, not forever refused.

prompt: Automated external defibrillation is indicated for:

options.text: All patients with sinus bradycardia and a strong pulse
Pulseless ventricular fibrillation / pulseless ventricular tachycardia (shockable rhythms)
Every stable patient with chest pain
Conscious patients with normal mentation and pulse

explanation: AEDs shock shockable arrest rhythms; pulsed patients are not defibrillated.

distractorExplanations.a: Bradycardia with pulse needs other care.
distractorExplanations.c: Stable chest pain is not AED shock.
distractorExplanations.d: Conscious pulsed patients are not defibrillated.

prompt: High-quality adult CPR compression depth is generally:

options.text: Less than 1 cm to avoid injury always
As deep as possible through the spine
At least 2 inches (5 cm) with full chest recoil
Only enough to move clothing

explanation: Adequate depth and recoil improve coronary perfusion; excessive unsafe force is not the goal—meet guideline depth.

distractorExplanations.b: Compressing through the spine is harmful.
distractorExplanations.d: Clothing movement is not a depth measure.

prompt: After an AED delivers a shock, the EMT should next:

options.text: Check a pulse for a full 3 minutes before any compressions
Turn off the AED permanently
Give the patient water
Resume CPR immediately for about 2 minutes (or until prompted), then reanalyze

explanation: Post-shock CPR minimizes interruptions; AEDs prompt the next analyze cycle.

distractorExplanations.c: Oral intake is inappropriate in arrest.

prompt: Which finding is most consistent with right-sided heart failure?

options.text: Peripheral edema and jugular venous distention
Only unilateral wheezes from a foreign body
Isolated ankle sprain
Normal exam with marathon tolerance suddenly

explanation: Systemic venous congestion signs suggest right-sided failure; correlate with history.

prompt: Aspirin is given in suspected ACS (per protocol) primarily because it:

options.text: Dissolves established fibrin clots like fibrinolytics in the field always
Inhibits platelet aggregation and may reduce clot progression
Raises blood sugar
Replaces the need for EMS transport

explanation: Antiplatelet effect is the early ACS benefit; fibrinolytics/PCI are separate hospital decisions.

distractorExplanations.a: EMTs do not give field fibrinolytics as aspirin’s role.
distractorExplanations.c: Aspirin is not a glucose agent.

prompt: A patient with a VAD (ventricular assist device) is unresponsive and pulseless by usual means. The EMT should:

options.text: Assume death without assessment because VADs prevent all emergencies
Defibrillate by placing pads only on the device driveline
Follow local VAD/cardiac arrest protocols (often CPR if indicated) and contact medical direction/device coordinator resources
Give oral nitroglycerin

explanation: VAD patients need specialized protocols; lack of palpable pulse may be expected—use device assessment cues and local rules.

distractorExplanations.b: Pads go on skin per AED/manual guidance, not on drivelines.
distractorExplanations.d: Unresponsive patients do not take oral NTG.

prompt: The primary survey in trauma follows which priority concept?

options.text: Find and fix life threats to airway, breathing, and circulation first
Complete SAMPLE history before any exam
Splint every fracture before airway care
Transport only after full secondary survey always

explanation: XABC/ABC priorities address immediate killers first.

distractorExplanations.d: Load-and-go may interrupt full secondary survey.

prompt: A sucking chest wound (open pneumothorax) is initially managed by:

options.text: Deep abdominal thrusts
Occlusive dressing sealed on three sides (or commercial chest seal) per protocol
Removing the occlusive dressing after each breath forever
Encouraging the patient to lie prone flat always

explanation: Three-sided occlusive dressing can prevent air entry while allowing escape; monitor for tension.

distractorExplanations.c: Indiscriminate removal without indication is wrong.
distractorExplanations.d: Positioning is individualized; prone is not default.

prompt: External arterial bleeding that is life-threatening should be controlled by:

options.text: Ice packs alone
Trendelenburg position only
Direct pressure and tourniquet use when indicated for extremities
Warm compresses and observation

explanation: Massive hemorrhage control is a top trauma priority; tourniquets save lives in extremity bleeding.

distractorExplanations.a: Ice is insufficient for arterial spurting.
distractorExplanations.b: Position alone does not stop arterial bleeding.

prompt: A patient with paradoxical chest wall movement after blunt trauma likely has:

options.text: Simple ankle sprain
Isolated nosebleed
Migraine headache
Flail chest

explanation: Flail segment moves paradoxically and impairs ventilation; stabilize and support breathing.

prompt: For a suspected pelvic fracture with shock, EMS should:

options.text: Avoid log-rolling excessively, stabilize pelvis per protocol, and treat for shock
Have the patient walk to assess stability
Rock the pelvis repeatedly to confirm fracture
Apply ice and discharge on scene

explanation: Pelvic fractures can cause massive bleeding; minimize movement and stabilize.

prompt: Burn severity estimation in the field commonly uses:

options.text: Only the patient's subjective pain score
Rule of Nines (or age-appropriate alternative) for BSA percentage
Pupil size exclusively
Blood glucose only

explanation: TBSA estimation guides fluid/transport decisions along with depth and location.

prompt: A penetrating object impaled in the thigh should generally be:

options.text: Removed immediately to reduce infection
Twisted out to free clothing
Stabilized in place and not removed in the field unless it interferes with CPR/airway
Cut flush with the skin always

explanation: Impaled objects may tamponade bleeding; stabilize for transport unless removal is required for resuscitation.

distractorExplanations.d: Cutting flush still leaves the object and may worsen control issues.

prompt: Cushing's triad (late increased ICP sign) includes:

options.text: Hypotension, tachycardia, and fever only
Hypoglycemia, polyuria, polydipsia
Wheezing, hives, and stridor
Hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respirations

explanation: Cushing response is a late brain herniation warning; manage airway and rapid transport.

distractorExplanations.a: That pattern fits other shock/infection states.

prompt: Spinal motion restriction is most clearly indicated when:

options.text: Mechanism and exam suggest possible spinal injury (e.g., high-risk MOI with midline tenderness/neuro deficits)
Every patient with a hangnail
Only patients under age 10
Never in modern EMS

explanation: Selective spinal precautions follow protocol based on MOI and findings.

distractorExplanations.b: Minor injuries without risk factors do not mandate full SMR.
distractorExplanations.c: Age alone is not the rule.
distractorExplanations.d: SMR remains indicated when criteria met.

prompt: Evisceration management includes:

options.text: Replace organs firmly into the abdomen on scene
Cover protruding organs with moist sterile dressing and occlusive cover; do not push back inside
Apply a dry adhesive bandage tightly over organs
Give the patient food and water

explanation: Keep organs moist and protected; replacement is a surgical task.

distractorExplanations.a: Pushing organs back can worsen injury/contamination.

prompt: A patient with a penetrating chest wound develops worsening dyspnea and JVD. You should suspect:

options.text: Isolated ankle sprain
Hyperventilation syndrome only
Tension pneumothorax physiology and support ABC/ALS per protocol
Food poisoning

explanation: Penetrating chest trauma with distress/JVD raises tension pneumothorax concern—rapid transport/ALS.

distractorExplanations.d: GI illness does not explain this trauma pattern.

prompt: For an evisceration, appropriate EMT care includes:

options.text: Replace organs firmly into the abdomen at the scene
Wipe organs with alcohol pads aggressively
Give the patient a large meal
Cover with a moist sterile dressing and occlusive covering as trained; do not push organs back

explanation: Keep organs moist and protected; reduction is a surgical task.

distractorExplanations.a: Do not replace eviscerated organs in the field.

prompt: A traction splint is generally indicated for:

options.text: Isolated midshaft femur fracture without contraindications per protocol
Pelvic fracture as the only treatment
Open skull fracture
Suspected cervical spine injury alone

explanation: Traction splints stabilize midshaft femurs when criteria are met; pelvis/spine need other care.

distractorExplanations.b: Pelvis needs binder/stabilization strategies, not classic traction splint as sole care.

prompt: Cushing's triad (late increased ICP sign) includes:

options.text: Hypotension, tachycardia, and warm dry skin only
Hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respirations
Fever, rash, and joint pain only
Polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia only

explanation: Rising ICP can produce hypertension with reflex bradycardia and abnormal breathing.

distractorExplanations.a: That pattern fits shock more than Cushing.

prompt: When managing an amputation, you should:

options.text: Place the part in water to soak
Throw the part away to reduce scene time
Control bleeding, care for the stump, and transport the part wrapped cool/dry per protocol (not directly on ice)
Apply a tourniquet to the amputated part instead of the stump

explanation: Save the part properly for possible reimplantation while prioritizing life-threatening hemorrhage.

distractorExplanations.d: Tourniquet goes on the residual limb as indicated.

prompt: SAMPLE history stands for:

options.text: Signs/Symptoms, Allergies, Medications, Past history, Last oral intake, Events
Saturation, Airway, Medications, Pulse, LOC, ECG
Stroke, Asthma, MI, PE, Lung, ED
Scene, Ambulance, Medic, Patient, Landing, Exit

explanation: SAMPLE structures medical history gathering.

prompt: OPQRST is primarily used to assess:

options.text: Trauma scene hazards only
Pain/symptom characteristics (Onset, Provocation, Quality, Radiation, Severity, Time)
Only pediatric developmental milestones
Ambulance maintenance schedules

explanation: OPQRST fleshes out the chief complaint symptom profile.

prompt: A patient with stroke-like symptoms should be transported with priority on:

options.text: Waiting on scene for symptoms to resolve for hours
Giving oral aspirin crushed in juice always
Rapid transport to an appropriate stroke-capable facility within time windows
Encouraging ambulation upstairs for assessment

explanation: Time-sensitive stroke care needs rapid identification and transport; avoid delays and unsafe oral meds if swallow uncertain.

distractorExplanations.b: Aspirin is not routine for suspected stroke in field like ACS.

prompt: Hypoglycemia in a conscious patient who can swallow may be treated with:

options.text: Insulin injection by EMT basic without protocol
Large IV potassium bolus
Withholding all sugar indefinitely
Oral glucose per protocol

explanation: Oral glucose is standard for symptomatic hypoglycemia when airway is protected.

distractorExplanations.a: EMT scope/protocols vary; insulin is not typical BLS.

prompt: Anaphylaxis treatment priority medication in EMS protocols is typically:

options.text: Epinephrine
Oral antihistamine only as sole therapy
Aspirin
Nitroglycerin

explanation: IM epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis; antihistamines are adjuncts.

distractorExplanations.b: Antihistamines alone are insufficient for anaphylaxis.

prompt: Which best describes implied consent?

options.text: Any bystander can refuse care for an adult patient
Unresponsive or impaired patients are treated under assumption they would want life-saving care
Consent is never needed in EMS
Minors always refuse for themselves in all states

explanation: Implied consent covers emergency care when a patient cannot decide; competent adults may refuse.

distractorExplanations.a: Bystanders cannot refuse for competent adults.

prompt: During extrication, the EMS provider's first responsibility is:

options.text: Entering an unstable vehicle immediately without assessment
Turning off traffic lights citywide
Scene safety and personal protective measures before patient access
Pronouncing death before arrival

explanation: Rescuer safety enables sustained patient care; hazards must be controlled.

distractorExplanations.a: Rushing into unstable scenes creates more victims.
distractorExplanations.b: Traffic control is coordinated, not citywide light control by EMT.
distractorExplanations.d: Death pronouncement rules vary and follow assessment.

prompt: A DNR order is generally valid when:

options.text: A neighbor says the patient 'wouldn't want care' without documentation
The patient is awake and requesting full treatment
Any faded tattoo that says DNR
It meets local legal requirements and is presented/verified per protocol

explanation: Follow legal advance directives/DNR documentation; honor patient wishes when they request care and are competent.

distractorExplanations.b: Competent requests for care supersede outdated assumptions.
distractorExplanations.c: Tattoos are not reliable legal DNRs.

prompt: Seizure management priorities include:

options.text: Protect from injury, maintain airway, and time the seizure; do not force objects into the mouth
Place a spoon between the teeth
Restrain arms rigidly to the bed frame
Give oral water during convulsions

explanation: Protective positioning and airway support; never insert objects into the mouth.

distractorExplanations.d: Oral intake during seizure risks aspiration.

prompt: Which statement about ambulance emergency driving is correct?

options.text: Red lights allow running any red signal at full speed without slowing
Lights/sirens do not guarantee right-of-way; drive with due regard for safety
Seat belts are optional for providers in the back
Speed limits never apply to EMS

explanation: Emergency privilege requires due regard; crashes during response harm everyone.

distractorExplanations.d: Traffic laws still constrain unsafe driving.

prompt: Which patient is the highest triage priority in a multi-casualty incident (START concept)?

options.text: A walking wounded patient with a minor laceration
A patient with an isolated finger sprain who is ambulatory
An unresponsive patient with inadequate breathing after airway positioning attempts fail
A deceased patient with dependent lividity and obvious mortal injuries

explanation: Immediate (red) patients have life threats; expectant/dead and minor walking wounded are lower priority.

distractorExplanations.b: Ambulatory minor injuries are low acuity.
distractorExplanations.d: Expectant/dead are not treated first in MCI triage.

prompt: Oral glucose is indicated for a diabetic patient who:

options.text: Is unresponsive with no gag reflex
Has hyperglycemia with Kussmaul respirations only
Has a known allergy to glucose and refuses care
Is hypoglycemic (or suspected) and can protect their airway/swallow

explanation: Oral sugar requires intact airway protective reflexes; unresponsive patients need other glucose routes/ALS.

distractorExplanations.a: Aspiration risk if unable to swallow.

prompt: A patient with suspected stroke within the window should be transported:

options.text: Promptly to an appropriate stroke-capable facility with prenotification when possible
Home for observation only
To the farthest hospital to avoid busy EDs
After waiting several hours to see if symptoms resolve

explanation: Time-sensitive stroke care needs rapid transport and hospital alert.

prompt: Which is a common early sign of compensated shock?

options.text: Bradycardia with flushed dry skin and hypertension always
Tachycardia with cool, pale, diaphoretic skin while blood pressure is still near normal
Normal mentation with bounding pulses and no distress always
Isolated pruritus without vital changes

explanation: Compensation often raises HR and causes vasoconstriction signs before hypotension appears.

distractorExplanations.a: That pattern is not classic hypovolemic compensation.

prompt: When documenting a patient refusal, the EMT should:

options.text: Have a minor sibling sign for an adult without authority
Skip explaining risks to save time
Ensure the patient is informed of risks, is competent, document thoroughly, and involve medical direction per protocol
Force treatment after a competent informed refusal

explanation: Informed refusal requires capacity, risk disclosure, and solid documentation.

distractorExplanations.d: Competent refusals are respected within law/protocol.
